. The Chaldeans are to destroy the Moabites. We should be thankful that we are required to seek the salvation of people's lives and the salvation of their souls, not to shed their blood; but we will be even more without excuse if we do this pleasant work deceitfully. The cities will be laid in ruins, and the country will be wasted. There will be great sorrow. There will be great hurry. If anyone could give wings to sinners, still they could not fly out of the reach of Divine indignation. There are many who persist in unrepented sin, yet long enjoy outward prosperity. They had been long corrupt and unreformed, secure and sensual in prosperity. They have no changes to their peace and prosperity, therefore their hearts and lives are unchanged.

. The destruction of Moab is further prophesied, to awaken them by national repentance and reformation to prevent the trouble, or by a personal repentance and reformation to prepare for it. In reading this long list of threats, and meditating on the terror, it will be more useful to us to keep in view the power of God’s anger and the terror of his judgments, and to have our hearts possessed with a holy awe of God and of his wrath, than to search into all the figures and expressions here used. Yet it is not perpetual destruction. The chapter ends with a promise of their return out of captivity in the latter days. Even with Moabites God will not contend forever, nor be always angry. The Jews refer it to the days of the Messiah; then the captives of the Gentiles, under the yoke of sin and Satan, shall be brought back by Divine grace, which will make them free indeed.
